related_experience__training.experiences_skills_qualifications_relate:
Over the past eight years, I have worked directly with adolescents and young adults in both educational and community settings, with a consistent focus on academic support, social-emotional development, and college/career readiness. Most recently, I have been employed as a Student Support Specialist at Rainier Valley Youth Center, where I coordinated after-school tutoring, led small social skills groups, and provided one-on-one mentoring for students who were identified as at-risk due to attendance, academic performance, or behavioral concerns.

In that role, I routinely collaborated with school counselors, teachers, and families to create individualized support plans. For example, I worked closely with a ninth-grade student who was failing English and Algebra and had missed over 20 days of school. I coordinated weekly check-ins with the student, established a simple tracking system for missing assignments, and facilitated a meeting between the student, his guardian, and his teachers. Over the course of one semester, his attendance improved to over 90%, and he raised both course grades from an F to a C+. This experience deepened my skills in building rapport with students who are initially resistant to help, as well as in using data (grades, attendance, behavior reports) to guide interventions.

I am also very comfortable designing and delivering workshops and small-group sessions. At Rainier Valley Youth Center, I developed a six-week curriculum called "Foundations of Study Skills" that covered time management, note-taking strategies, test preparation, and goal setting. I piloted this program with a cohort of 15 tenth graders identified by their teachers as needing additional academic support. Pre- and post-surveys showed that participants reported increased confidence in organizing their schoolwork, and teachers reported fewer missing assignments from those students. I adapted the curriculum each term based on feedback, which helped me learn to be flexible and responsive to student needs rather than rigidly attached to a pre-set plan.

My counseling graduate training has given me strong foundational skills in active listening, motivational interviewing, and crisis response. During my practicum at Roosevelt Middle School, I co-facilitated a grief support group for students who had experienced recent losses and provided short-term, solution-focused counseling for students dealing with anxiety, peer conflict, and family stressors. I learned the importance of maintaining clear boundaries, documenting sessions accurately and promptly, and following school protocols for mandated reporting and safety assessments. I am familiar with working under the supervision of a licensed counselor, receiving feedback, and integrating that feedback into my practice.

I am also very comfortable working with families from diverse cultural and linguistic backgrounds. I grew up in a bilingual household (English and Spanish) and have volunteered for over three years with a community organization that supports Latino families navigating the school system. I have translated at parent-teacher conferences, helped families understand individualized education plans (IEPs), and facilitated workshops on how to support children’s homework routines at home. These experiences have helped me develop a culturally responsive approach and a deep appreciation for the strengths that families bring, even when they may feel uncertain about their own familiarity with the school system.

Additionally, I am organized and detail-oriented, which is crucial when managing multiple students’ needs, documentation requirements, and communication with staff and families. I am comfortable using student information systems (such as Skyward) to track attendance and grades, and I regularly use spreadsheets and shared documents to keep track of student caseloads, intervention plans, and progress notes. I am also proactive about communication; for example, I send follow-up emails after meetings summarizing key points and next steps, which has helped reduce confusion and ensure accountability.

Finally, I genuinely enjoy working with young people and see their potential even when they are struggling. Former students have told me that what they appreciated most was that I remembered the small details of their lives (like their favorite hobbies or concerns about younger siblings) and consistently checked in, even when there was no immediate crisis. I believe this kind of consistent presence and reliability is essential in a role supporting students’ academic and personal growth, and it is something I am committed to bringing to this position.
related_experience__training.other_specialized_or_professional_traini:
• Mental Health First Aid (Youth): Completed an 8-hour Youth Mental Health First Aid certification course in March 2023, offered through the National Council for Mental Wellbeing. Training covered how to identify, understand, and respond to signs of mental health and substance use challenges among adolescents, including how to approach a student in distress, assess for immediate risk, and connect them with appropriate professional help. Certificate received and renewed as required.

• Trauma-Informed Schools Training: Participated in a 16-hour professional development series in 2022 focused on trauma-informed practices in educational settings. Topics included the impact of adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) on learning and behavior, strategies for de-escalation, creating predictable classroom routines, and working collaboratively with teachers to support students with trauma histories. I applied these strategies by helping one teacher redesign her classroom expectations and transition routines, which resulted in fewer behavior referrals for two students with known trauma histories.

• Restorative Practices in Schools Workshop: Completed a 12-hour workshop on restorative practices, including community circles, restorative conversations, and re-entry meetings after suspensions. I have since co-facilitated restorative circles to address peer conflicts and minor bullying incidents, focusing on helping students take responsibility for their actions and repair harm rather than simply receive punitive consequences.

• Suicide Prevention and Intervention (QPR Gatekeeper Training): Completed QPR (Question, Persuade, Refer) Gatekeeper Training in 2021. This training strengthened my ability to recognize warning signs of suicidal ideation, ask direct and clear questions about self-harm, and connect students to appropriate crisis resources. Although I am not a licensed clinician, this training has helped me respond more confidently and appropriately in situations where students express hopelessness or self-harm thoughts and to follow school protocols carefully.

• College and Career Readiness Workshops: Attended a three-day training in 2020 on college and career advising for first-generation students, sponsored by the Washington Student Achievement Council. Topics included interpreting high school graduation requirements, understanding financial aid (FAFSA/WAFSA), supporting students in writing personal statements, and exploring non-traditional pathways such as apprenticeships and community college certificate programs. I used this training to build a simple step-by-step checklist for juniors and seniors at Rainier Valley Youth Center, which I walked through individually with students and their families.

• Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Training: Participated in ongoing DEI trainings through my current employer, including workshops on implicit bias, microaggressions, and culturally responsive communication. One specific training, "Culturally Responsive Engagement with Immigrant and Refugee Families," was particularly impactful; it provided concrete strategies for avoiding deficit-based language, recognizing cultural strengths, and adjusting meeting formats to be more accessible and welcoming to families whose first language is not English.

• Technology and Data Systems: Completed internal training on using the Skyward student information system and Google Workspace for Education (Google Classroom, Docs, Sheets, and Forms). I regularly use these tools to monitor grades and attendance, share resources with students, and collect feedback on group sessions. I also completed a short online course on basic data analysis in Excel, which has helped me create simple dashboards to track student progress over time.

Collectively, these trainings have given me a strong practical toolkit that I use daily in my work with students, families, and school staff. They have also reinforced the importance of continuous professional learning, especially in areas like trauma-informed care, DEI, and mental health, where best practices continue to evolve.
